speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 14, 1996. Survivor is Abraham Bomba. The interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Monticello in the state of New York. The country is United States of America. And the language is in English. My name is Louise Bobrow, B-O-B-R-O-W. Today's date is August 14, 1996. I am conducting an interview with survivor Abraham Bomba. The interview is being conducted in Monticello, New York in the United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Abraham Bomba, B-O-M-B-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames or any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is June the 9th, 1913.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your present age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 83 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Beuthen Oberschlesien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-U-T-H-E-N. That's the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me approximately where this is located in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is located on the border of Poland, next to Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing-- is it where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in Poland, in Czenstochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Czenstochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-Z-E-N-S-T-O-C-H-O-W-A. That's in Polish spelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child, growing up in Czenstochowa, how would you describe it? What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like-- just like any other city in Poland. There were all kind people, all kind organization. Mostly which I attended was Jewish places, Jewish organizations. We went to school over there. We had special public school for Jewish children and for non-Jewish, which I attended the Jewish schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that have a name, the school that you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the public school from that city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grades did you attend there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you also get a Jewish education in this school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Before noon-- that mean from 8:00 to about 12:00 we went to cheder, which is a Jewish educational school. We learned of the Bible, or other study of the Jewish history and of the Jewish Talmud.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in the afternoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In afternoon, I went to a public school, which started at 1 o'clock-- 1 o'clock. And it finished sometimes seven, sometimes 8 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czenstochowa, at the time you were growing up, what was the entire size of the population approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: The entire population, it was around 135,000. And Jewish people was around 35,000, 38,000 at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the Jewish population congregated in mainly one area or were they scattered throughout?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were scattered, but not in a big group. Mostly the Jewish area was the lower part of the city, in another word also the poorest part of the city in Czenstochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the section where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the street that you lived on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning was under whatever it is called. [INAUDIBLE]. Now, that mean under water. And then I lived on [INAUDIBLE], which is mean under old marketplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your house looked like? Was it a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, describe-- there's nothing much to describe. It was a very small apartment, very small apartment. As a matter of fact, there was only one room, the whole apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people lived in the one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many people lived in that apartment? Five. We were from the one, not the rich one, not the fortunate one, but the working class, where we couldn't afford any better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernard. In Jewish, [? Bertle. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bomba.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a helper manufacturing shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have his own business or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --he worked for someone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked for somebody. He hardly, he hardly worked, because it was hard to get a job, especially in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe him? What was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very intelligent man, I would say. Fluently in German, Polish, Jewish, Russian. And he was a very nice looking man. I would say so. And he-- he was. Also, he spent seven and a half years in the Russia army at that time. So he was somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any special memory of anything you might have done with him? Does anything stand out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't say special. The only thing is that he taught me how to behave, how to be a human being, how to get education. Even-- he said, I work like that-- even when you don't keep it, like religion or other thing, but you should know. When you come together with people, you should know how to open up your mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Touba. Touba and Feigl. But we called her Touba. Well, everybody called her Touba.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Kozuch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she never worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very hard for me to describe her, because she was a lovely, nice looking woman. And she was the real Jewish mother. Like to say, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I mean, she did everything for the children. You know, I remember-- I think she was at her years, which when she passed away, she was almost 50 years old. And when we-- in the house, we were little, I mean, 12, 13 years old, she wouldn't let nobody to do anything she did, like bringing in wood, coal, other thing. She would do it. And she was something special in cooking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really special in cooking. She could make from potatoes in water, she could make a meal like for a king. I don't know how she did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a favorite recipe of hers that she made that you love? What did you love?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything she make.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything come to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything she maked, which wasn't too much to make. It was many, many nights we went to sleep without food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a brother and sister in Paris. I never met them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened that my father's first wife passed away. She was sick. She had cancer. And after quite a time-- I don't know exactly when-- he met my mother. And they married, and I was the one who was born from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names, the brother and sister in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. His name was Henry, and her name is Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last name was Bomba. Her name was Chatkine after her marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have other brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had another brother, Jacob, and a younger brother, Judah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were with your-- your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you recall any special memories that you had with these brothers in Czenstochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was a little older than I was. He was a very nice gentleman. He worked very hard to help out in the house, to bring in-- because when my father passed away, we were almost children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when your father passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 16 years. And we had a little boy also. He was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six or seven years old. And hardly was food in the house. So when he started to grown up, and he went to work, so he helped out a lot in the house with everything. When he started working, we had almost everything in the house. I don't mean everything, you know, like comfortable. But at least we had food in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just your mother and the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: None of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I'm named after my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avrum Leib. That's my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your religious background when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was growing up, I was, according to my father, I wasn't too religious, but I know the Bible. I know the Bible by heart and still up to now. The thing is I grew up to be a Jew, a good Jew, a fair Jew, but to be a Jew. I never, never in my life thought that being in a party, like anti-religious party or a Communist Party or the Socialist Party, would give us Jews any survival or any help. One thing we could do is we've got to have our own state. And that is the state of Israel, which since I was, I would say, 10 or 12 years, I belong to-- to the Youth Organization of the Zionist. And then I belonged to the Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of those organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the organization was Poale Zion, which today they don't exist. But they got their name in Israel, Mapai, which the Ben-Gurion organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were active in this as a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was active in that organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any Jewish holidays that you celebrated in your home? For example, do you remember-- did you celebrate Shabbos on Friday nights?

speaker: interviewee
text: All. All the holidays, not only Shabbos, every holiday. A holiday in our house was a real holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbos like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Shabbos was a special day. Nobody went to work. We would dress in the best clothes, what we had. We went to shul. From the shul, we came home. We ate whatever we got. And then, in the afternoon sometime, we were-- we went to cheder or we went to shul to say a special prayer. Saturday afternoon, there's a special prayer we say-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] means how to behave.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbos meal like Friday night? What'd your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She tried to make a meal. It should be different from the whole week, if it was money-wise. But she always did something, depends on what she could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall anything, what you used to eat on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what do you eat?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: The most important thing was a cholent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the cholent? What did it consist of?

speaker: interviewee
text: What come, potatoes, meat, a bone, a little fattening. And we were waiting for that for the Saturday meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she-- when did she start cooking it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Friday afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when would you actually eat it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saturday, after 1:00, 2 o'clock, after we came from the shul. And it was prepared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she bake her own challah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You didn't buy any challah. At that time, we didn't know about the-- had to make-- make challah or to go to-- everyone did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about Pesach? Do you remember anything about Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Pesach was a special holiday for Jewish people, not only for myself, but for all the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was something, which is like today to see that would be like a dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because first of all, when my father was alive-- that was the really thing-- he was dressed like a king.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: White. White-- like with a white garment all of it. And special place to sit, a special-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how you call-- utilities to prepare the food, because there was-- it has to be kosher, the Pesach. It cannot be the same what you use the whole year around.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the matzah? How was that prepared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was baked by bakers. We bought it. Nobody did for themself, because that's a tough job to do and also not allowed to do. It has to be by a rabbi, special, the oven and the place where they baked it. No private, nobody did, not any that I know of. Nobody did. We had bakeries that did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your house prepared in a special way for Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was special cleaned, special painted for it. [? Drink. ?] So it should look different than the whole year around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a special job when it came to Pesach that you were in charge of? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special. I also made food and prepared this. We go to bring water in, because we didn't have the water in the house, like they have it now. And it was a job also to bring it over, a job to prepare the oven. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other holidays that you recall? For example, Sukkot. Did you have a sukkah in your-- in your yard or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had that. It was like from the people that lived in the same building, which wasn't too big. It was about six, seven tenants in the same building. We had a small sukkah. And we had it over there. So we ate in that sukkah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, favorite holiday, I would say, Rosh Hashanah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was my favorite, because it was the new year. The new year starts. And the joy between people. It was wishing everybody a good year. That was-- that was a favorite, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- I had not a huge bar mitzvah, because we were poor people. We didn't celebrate it. My father took me in to the shul. And I make-- there wasn't even on a Saturday. And it wasn't-- just he took me in. He showed me how to put on tefillin, if you know what that means.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me. What does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is, you know, they put around. It make from leather, and also on the hat, and make the [NON-ENGLISH]. And that's all it was. I-- I don't even remember [? oh ?] we had whiskey or something to it, because we had no money for it. That was the bar mitzvah, not the bar mitzvah that is today, which cost-- [INAUDIBLE] I would say-- $10,000, $20,000, $50,000, [? using ?] with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the Jewish community of Czenstochowa? Besides yeshiva and-- how many synagogues would you say were in Czenstochowa when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had synagogue, quite a few. But mostly they prayed is in shtiblach.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's shtiebel?

speaker: interviewee
text: What shtiblach means, a little prayer house. Not exactly a synagogue, a little prayer house, private. People were there, two or three a room. They converted one room for the Torah and for the private prayers in the evening. In mine little house, where I lived, between seven tenants, there was also a little shtiebel, which we made the prayers Friday night, Saturday in that place. But we had synagogues, beautiful synagogues. One of the most beautiful was the-- we called it the German synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it called the German one?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's the name was all the time. Why? I don't know. Where for holiday, at that time, people used to come with horses and wagon-- [INAUDIBLE] beautiful wagon. [INAUDIBLE] they called it-- to the synagogue. High cylinder hats. Very, very rich, very famous people. As a matter of fact, today, they make, from that synagogue, a museum, and also a opera house from that right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where it was standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in-- in Czenstochowa, same place. Then we had a synagogue. They called it the old synagogue. The old synagogue was for people, common people, not the very rich, not the very poor. Over there, we had special canters. And it was make something, something like a dream. I got a friend of mine, which I will talk about him later, who was with me in Treblinka, his father painted it. He took eight years. Not just painting. No, the pictures created on the ceiling and on the walls, they were masterpieces. It's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that still standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was destroyed in the first days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jewish institutions in Czenstochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Theatre, newspaper? What-- what was there? What else was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had institution to help poor people. We had doctors. Somebody was sick, they went to, didn't have to pay anything, with medicine. We had institution like very, very poor people, they couldn't afford to make a meal out. So we had an institution that supported them, that brought in food for them. We had also an institution to marry off young girls, which they had no diary-- dowry. And they took-- I don't know from where-- and they clothed them, and they married them off, which call, in our language, Hachnasat Cala, to bring in the [? caller ?] that she shouldn't be ashamed, as your married. Then we had also an institution for a few people. They had no place where to sleep. They call it Hachnasat Orchim That is, I guess, people that came from somewhere, people, beggars. They have no place where to go. They slept over there, and they had their meals over there. It was supported by the Jewish people, by the richer Jewish people, who could afford. And there was also sent-- set a tax from the kehilla. Kehilla means the committee of the Jewish people. They taxed the people-- they know who so much and so much-- to help out the poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends growing up in Czenstochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had-- I had a Jewish friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your relationships like with the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: My relation-- my special relation was very good, excellent was. Before the war, I had a lot of friends. We went out together. And even I will tell a story about the Holocaust. I will tell you the story about non-Jewish people, which I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally experience any anti-Semitism prior to the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The-- the Polish people, which they didn't live in our section, in the Jewish section mostly. There were-- no, we had fights with them, throwing stones, and calling names each other. Oh, we went through-- we went through that, because they were agitated by priest, by teachers, anti-Semitic. And I would say it wasn't even the fault of the kids. They heard of it. And they didn't understand anything else. But the Jewish people killed Jesus, which nobody of us know what that is. And from that, it started, the anti-Semitism about the young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself as a youngster? What were you like? What was your personality like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It's hard for me to describe myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you outgoing? Were you shy? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was more than the shy side. Yeah, I was more shy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for activities of fun with your friends? What were you involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I involved in-- in sport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly soccer games. That most-- most of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any dreams or ambitions of what you wanted to be when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could never an ambition, because I know mine ambition will not go through, because I couldn't afford it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had ambition.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that ambition?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had ambition to be a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family have access to a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was going on as Hitler rose to power inside of Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The papers. We had papers with everything. And then in the '30s, then we had radio around also. Even in 1932, '33, we had a radio in our own house. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents discuss what they were listening to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my-- my parent-- my father was not alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- she did not too much about it, because for a woman, politics-- for a Jewish woman, especially, it was the high class to discuss politic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your thoughts were about hearing-- you heard about Hitler, you said. What-- what were you thinking. As he was rising to power, what were your thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I tell you. I was thinking that they were talking a lot, but actually they would not do what they did, because we couldn't believe it that they're gonna to do to kill people, to cremate people, or-- or poison them or kill them. Which is in this book, what he wrote, being in jail in 1923-- he was jailed. He wrote that book in jail. So actually, we didn't-- we didn't think too much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things begin to change for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It began to change in the late '30s. I tell you why. Because the Polish government and the Polish people, they leaned on the German and the Nazis. And their word was like that, just wait until Hitler will come. Then we will teach you a lessons, to the Jewish people. So that changed altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop this tape now. We will continue shortly.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you wish.
